name: Bourgogne
style_notes: The regional Bourgogne appellation covers the full extent of the Burgundy wine region. Reds are based on Pinot Noir — typically lighter, more approachable expressions compared to village and premier cru levels. Whites from Chardonnay offer clean, mineral-driven fruit.
vintage_notes: Quality varies widely with vintage conditions. The continental climate means cool springs can threaten yields while warm, dry summers produce concentrated fruit. 2015, 2019, and 2020 are standout recent years.

name: Côte de Nuits
style_notes: The northern half of the Côte d'Or, running south from Dijon. Home to Burgundy's greatest red wines: Gevrey-Chambertin, Morey-Saint-Denis, Chambolle-Musigny, Vougeot, Vosne-Romanée, and Nuits-Saint-Georges. The finest vineyards sit on the mid-slope, where iron-rich limestone clay produces wines of extraordinary depth and longevity.
vintage_notes: Pinot Noir here demands warm autumns to achieve full ripeness. Excessive rain at harvest is the perennial enemy. The best vintages — 2010, 2015, 2019, 2020 — combine warm growing seasons with dry harvests.

name: Gevrey-Chambertin
style_notes: The largest and arguably most celebrated village in the Côte de Nuits. Village wines are notably more structured and tannic than Chambolle or Vosne, reflecting the iron-rich limestone soils. Nine grand crus, including Chambertin and Chambertin-Clos de Bèze, sit at the heart of the village. Top producers: Rousseau, Dugat-Py, Trapet, Mortet.
vintage_notes: Gevrey's tannic structure means the best wines need a decade minimum to unwind. 2016, 2019, and 2015 produced wines of extraordinary concentration and aging potential. Avoid opening village-level wines before 8 years in top vintages.

name: Chambertin
style_notes: Napoleon's favourite wine. A 12.9-hectare grand cru site at the heart of Gevrey-Chambertin, producing some of Burgundy's most powerful and complex Pinot Noir. Wines of Chambertin combine the iron-fisted structure of the appellation with a rare perfume and silkiness that only emerges with age. Key owners: Rousseau, Leroy, Ponsot, Trapet.
vintage_notes: Chambertin demands patience above all else. Even in lighter vintages the wine rarely opens before 12–15 years. In exceptional vintages — 2010, 2015, 2019 — expect 30+ year aging potential. Never open a Rousseau Chambertin before 15 years without regret.

name: Pauillac
style_notes: The most prestigious commune in the Médoc, home to three first-growth châteaux: Lafite-Rothschild, Mouton-Rothschild, and Latour. The deep Quaternary gravel soils drain perfectly and warm rapidly, enabling Cabernet Sauvignon to ripen fully. Pauillac's signature: blackcurrant, cedar, cigar box, graphite — pure authority and precision.
vintage_notes: Pauillac rewards the patient collector above all Bordeaux communes. The 2016 and 2010 vintages are considered near-perfect. 2018, 2019, and 2015 are all exceptional. Lesser vintages like 2013 are approachable sooner but lack the structure for long aging.

name: Margaux
style_notes: The southernmost of the four great Médoc communes, known for producing the most feminine and perfumed red wines of Bordeaux. The fine, sandy gravel soils produce wines of extraordinary elegance: violet, cassis, dried rose, graphite. Château Margaux leads the appellation with wines of unrivalled delicacy and precision.
vintage_notes: Margaux's lighter soils ripen earlier than Pauillac, making it more consistent across vintages. The 2015 and 2016 vintages are exceptional. Earlier vintages from 1996, 2000, and 2005 remain outstanding for those with mature bottles.

name: Champagne
style_notes: The world's most celebrated sparkling wine region, 90 minutes northeast of Paris. The chalk subsoil is the defining feature: it retains water and reflects heat, producing high-acid, low-sugar base wines ideal for secondary fermentation. Non-vintage blends from the great houses — Krug, Bollinger, Pol Roger, Louis Roederer — demonstrate the pinnacle of consistent house style. Vintage Champagne is produced only in declared exceptional years.
vintage_notes: Vintage declarations are rare and significant: 2008, 2012, 2013, 2015, 2018, and 2019 are all outstanding. NV Champagne is designed for immediate drinking but prestige cuvées from exceptional vintages can age 20–30 years.

name: Sauternes
style_notes: The world's greatest sweet wine appellation, producing wines of extraordinary complexity and longevity from botrytis-affected Sémillon. Morning mists from the Ciron river encourage Botrytis cinerea (noble rot), which concentrates sugars and acids to extreme levels. Château d'Yquem is the sole Premier Cru Supérieur; its wines routinely outlive the people who buy them.
vintage_notes: Botrytis requires specific climatic conditions — warm, damp mornings and dry, sunny afternoons. Great Sauternes vintages: 2001 (legendary), 2009, 2011, 2013, 2015, 2017. Young Sauternes is drinkable but the best wines don't show their full complexity for 10–20 years.

name: Barolo
style_notes: The King of Italian wines. Barolo is 100% Nebbiolo grown in the hills south of Alba. The wine's paradox — light ruby color yet massive structure — is explained by Nebbiolo's unique aromatic intensity paired with very high tannin and acid. The two primary soil types define the two great styles: Tortonian marl from La Morra and Barolo communes produces earlier-maturing, more perfumed wines; Helvetian limestone-clay from Serralunga and Castiglione produces the most long-lived, powerful expressions.
vintage_notes: Barolo requires years of patience. 2010 and 2016 are considered transcendent — wines of century-aging potential. 2013 and 2015 are also exceptional. 2014 was a difficult, rain-affected year. Budget 15–25 years from the vintage for the finest single-vineyard wines.

name: Barbaresco
style_notes: Nebbiolo's more feminine expression. Neighboring Barolo but lighter in weight and often more approachable in youth. The three communes — Barbaresco, Neive, and Treiso — each produce distinct styles. Giacomo Conterno, Bruno Giacosa, Gaja, and Roagna are the defining names. Barbaresco's Tortonian marl produces more floral, red-fruit driven wines than the structured giants of Serralunga.
vintage_notes: Slightly earlier drinking than Barolo but still demands patience. 2016 is exceptional; 2010, 2013, and 2015 are outstanding. 2014 was challenging due to rain and rot pressure but careful producers made compelling wines from lower-yielding plots.

name: Chianti Classico
style_notes: The historic heart of Chianti, between Florence and Siena. Chianti Classico is 100% or near-100% Sangiovese in its purest form. The galestro and alberese soils produce wines of bright acidity, firm tannin, dried cherry, leather, and wild herbs. Gran Selezione represents single-vineyard or best-barrique bottlings aged minimum 30 months. Leading estates: Fontodi, Isole e Olena, Montevertine, Radda.
vintage_notes: Warmer vintages produce rounder, more hedonistic expressions; cooler years reveal Sangiovese's cerebral, food-friendly side. 2015, 2016, and 2019 are excellent. 2013 produced elegant, age-worthy wines. The Gran Selezione tier benefits from 10+ years of aging.

name: Brunello di Montalcino
style_notes: Italy's most age-worthy wine from a single-commune DOCG south of Siena. 100% Brunello (Sangiovese Grosso), minimum 5 years aging before release (6 for Riserva). The wines combine Sangiovese's signature acidity and tannin with the power and concentration that comes from Montalcino's warm, south-facing slopes. Biondi-Santi created the style; Soldera, Giacomo Conterno, and Poggio di Sotto are current benchmarks.
vintage_notes: The DOCG's strict release schedule means Brunello is always approaching maturity by the time it reaches shelves. 2010, 2012, 2015, and 2016 are outstanding. The 2015 Riservas are considered among the finest wines Italy has produced in decades.

name: Rioja
style_notes: Spain's most celebrated red wine region, straddling the Ebro valley. Three sub-zones: Rioja Alta, Rioja Alavesa (more Atlantic, higher altitude, limestone soils — the most elegant wines), and Rioja Oriental (warmer, more Mediterranean). Tempranillo dominates, blended with Garnacha for fruit, Mazuelo for color/tannin, and Graciano for acidity and perfume. Classification by aging: Joven, Crianza (2yr), Reserva (3yr), Gran Reserva (5yr). Extended American oak aging historically defined the style; shift toward French oak and single-vineyard expressions is ongoing.
vintage_notes: 2010, 2016, and 2019 are exceptional vintages across all sub-zones. 2015 and 2018 are outstanding. Gran Reserva from top years can cellar 20–30 years. Traditional Reserva is generally best 8–15 years from vintage.

name: Ribera del Duero
style_notes: Spain's most powerful Tempranillo-based wines, produced on the high plateau (750–900m) of the Duero valley. The extreme diurnal temperature range — scorching days and cold nights — preserves acidity while building concentration. The region is best known for Vega-Sicilia (Único), Pesquera, and Pingus. A modern, international style has emerged alongside the traditional; the best wines combine immense structure with elegance.
vintage_notes: The extreme continental climate makes vintage variation significant. 2012, 2016, and 2019 are outstanding. 2009 and 2010 produced powerful, classic wines. Top-tier wines (Único, Pingus) need 15+ years to show their full dimension.

name: Napa Valley
style_notes: The most celebrated wine region in the United States. The 30-mile valley, flanked by the Mayacamas and Vaca ranges, encompasses enormous climatic variation from the cool, fog-influenced southern end near San Pablo Bay to the warm northern reaches near Calistoga. Cabernet Sauvignon is the unrivalled king: full-bodied, rich, generously oaked, with ripe blackcurrant, plum, and cassis. The finest Napa Cabs — from Spring Mountain, Howell Mountain, Stags Leap, and Oakville — can rival Bordeaux first growths.
vintage_notes: Napa's Mediterranean climate produces reliably ripe vintages. 2013, 2016, 2018, and 2019 are exceptional. 2012 was outstanding. Even lesser years rarely disappoint given the region's consistent sunshine.

name: Stags Leap District
style_notes: The AVA that put Napa on the world map at the 1976 Paris Judgment. A narrow bench of volcanic tuff in the eastern Napa Valley, sheltered by the palisades from afternoon heat. Stags Leap District Cabernets are distinctive for their iron-fist-in-velvet-glove style: immense structure with a softness and elegance rare in Napa. Stag's Leap Wine Cellars Cask 23 and S.L.V., along with Shafer Hillside Select, define the house style.
vintage_notes: The volcanic soils and afternoon cooling moderate the warmest Napa vintages. Consistent across years. 2013, 2016, and 2018 are the recent standouts. Best wines need 10–20 years from release.

name: Sonoma Coast
style_notes: A large, geographically diverse AVA covering Sonoma County's Pacific coast. The true Sonoma Coast — the 'extreme' coastal sites around Freestone, Occidental, and the Fort Ross-Seaview sub-appellation — produces some of California's most Burgundian Pinot Noirs. Persistent fog, cold winds, and thin soils force vines to struggle, producing low yields of concentrated, high-acid fruit. Littorai, Peay, and Hirsch are the defining producers.
vintage_notes: The cool, maritime climate makes this a challenging region. 2012, 2013, 2016, and 2019 are the standouts. In warmer years the wines gain richness without losing their defining freshness. Extreme coastal sites can fail to ripen in the coldest years.

name: Willamette Valley
style_notes: Oregon's flagship wine region, stretching south from Portland. The cool, wet maritime climate produces Pinot Noirs that are often compared to Burgundy — high acidity, moderate alcohol, red fruit, and earthy complexity. The six sub-AVAs (Dundee Hills, Chehalem Mountains, Ribbon Ridge, Yamhill-Carlton, Eola-Amity Hills, McMinnville) each have distinct soil types and microclimates. Eyrie Vineyards put Oregon on the map in the 1980s; today Adelsheim, Elk Cove, Beaux Frères, and Lingua Franca lead.
vintage_notes: More vintage variation than California due to the rainy climate. 2012, 2014, 2016, and 2018 are outstanding. Wet years like 2011 challenged producers but yielded elegant, lower-alcohol wines from careful selection.

name: Mosel
style_notes: The Mosel is Riesling's spiritual home. Steep south-facing slate slopes above the meandering Mosel river create the conditions for Germany's most ethereal wines. The slate absorbs heat during the day and radiates it at night, enabling Riesling to ripen in what would otherwise be a marginal climate. Mosel Rieslings are defined by their piercing acidity, low alcohol, and the famous 'Schiefermineral' — the slaty, petrol-tinged minerality that is incomparable anywhere else on earth. Joh. Jos. Prüm, Egon Müller, Moselland, Clemens Busch, and Dönnhoff are the benchmarks.
vintage_notes: 2015, 2017, 2018, and 2019 are exceptional Mosel vintages. Riesling ages magnificently in the Mosel: a great GG Spätlese or Auslese can evolve for 30–50 years, passing through various stages of petrol and honey development.

name: Rheingau
style_notes: A small, prestigious Riesling region on the east-west stretch of the Rhine, between Hochheim and Rüdesheim. The south-facing slopes catch maximum sun while the Rhine moderates temperatures. Rheingau Rieslings are typically fuller-bodied and richer than Mosel, with more stone fruit and honey character alongside the distinctive mineral spine. Schloss Johannisberg (the oldest Riesling estate), Robert Weil, and Georg Breuer define the region's finest expressions.
vintage_notes: Generally reliable vintages due to the moderating Rhine influence. 2015, 2018, and 2019 are outstanding. Spätlese and Auslese bottlings from great years age beautifully for 20–30 years.

name: Douro
style_notes: The Douro Valley, carved by the Douro river into schist bedrock, is one of the world's most dramatic wine regions. Fortified Port — Vintage, LBV, Tawny, Colheita — is the region's historic pride; unfortified Douro reds of extraordinary quality have emerged since the 1990s. The schist soils force deep root systems, producing wines of remarkable concentration and minerality. Quinta do Crasto, Niepoort, Quinta do Vale Meão, and Ramos Pinto lead the table wine category. The Symington family (Graham's, Cockburn's, Dow's) and Fladgate (Taylor's, Fonseca) dominate vintage Port.
vintage_notes: Vintage Port declared years represent the apex of the Douro's potential. 2011, 2016, and 2017 are exceptional Port vintages. 2007, 2003, and 1994 were also outstanding. Vintage Port needs 20+ years minimum; the finest examples are still evolving at 50.

name: Alentejo
style_notes: Portugal's sun-drenched southern interior, producing generous, ripe, full-bodied reds and increasingly interesting whites. The Alentejo stretches south and east of Lisbon and accounts for a disproportionately large share of Portugal's premium wine exports. Modern Alentejo reds combine rich dark fruit — plum, blackberry — with the indigenous grapes' earthy, leathery character. Herdade do Esporão, Quinta do Crasto (also in Douro), and José Maria da Fonseca lead. Sub-zones Borba, Évora, and Portalegre produce the most distinguished wines.
vintage_notes: Hot vintages produce extracted, alcoholic wines; cooler years preserve freshness. 2017, 2019, and 2020 are outstanding recent vintages. The region's whites (Antão Vaz, Arinto blends) are best drunk young and fresh.

name: Barossa Valley
style_notes: Australia's most iconic wine region, home to some of the world's oldest Shiraz vines — bush vines planted in the 1840s–1880s that were never grafted onto phylloxera rootstock. Old-vine Barossa Shiraz is a category unto itself: immense concentration, richness, and complexity from tiny yields, with characteristic chocolate, licorice, ironstone, and leather. Penfolds Grange is produced here. Henschke (Hill of Grace), Torbreck, Two Hands, and Yalumba are key producers. Newer-wave producers (Chris Ringland, Seppeltsfield) push old-vine complexity further.
vintage_notes: 2010, 2012, 2016, and 2018 are the standout vintages. Old-vine Shiraz from exceptional years can age 25–40 years. The Barossa's warm climate produces consistent ripeness; variation comes from rainfall and harvest timing rather than ripeness failures.

name: Clare Valley
style_notes: Australia's greatest Riesling region, located north of Adelaide at 400–500m elevation. The high altitude and cool nights preserve acidity in a warm climate, producing Rieslings of startling freshness and structure. Clare Valley Riesling is always bone-dry, unlike German counterparts, with lime, lemon curd, and slate minerality — and extraordinary aging potential. Grosset Polish Hill and Watervale bottlings, Jim Barry Florita, and Knappstein are the benchmarks.
vintage_notes: Clare Valley Riesling ages magnificently under screwcap. 10-year-old wines develop extraordinary petrol and toast complexity while retaining freshness. 2014, 2016, and 2019 are outstanding.

name: Margaret River
style_notes: Western Australia's finest wine region, a narrow coastal strip south of Perth whose maritime climate closely mirrors Bordeaux. Margaret River Cabernet Sauvignon is arguably Australia's finest and most Bordeaux-like expression of the variety: cassis, graphite, dried herb, restrained oak. Chardonnay here is similarly precise and restrained. Cullen Wines (Diana Madeline), Vasse Felix, Moss Wood, and Cape Mentelle are the definitive estates.
vintage_notes: The maritime climate moderates vintage variation significantly. 2012, 2015, 2019, and 2021 are outstanding. Margaret River Cabernet benefits from 10–20 years of aging; great vintages can go beyond.
